visits.text: Morning light filters between 50-meter sandstone pillars like in an open-air cathedral. The Adršpach Rocks are Central Europe's largest sandstone rock labyrinth: narrow streets between cliffs, wooden bridges over ravines, black and calm water at the bottom of the Great Lake. The effect is genuinely disorienting.

A fire in 1824 burned the forest for several weeks. Until then, the rocks were masked by trees and the site little frequented. It was this forest accident that revealed the formations and triggered tourism: the first alpinists arrived from Saxony, and the first documented ascent of 'The King' dates to May 20, 1923.

The formations have names worth the detour: the Mayor and Mayoress, the Lovers, the Gothic Gate, the Sugar Loaf. Spot the Gothic Gate at the circuit entrance—it's the most photographed and narrowest passage of the site. Locals know others, unofficial ones, that the signs ignore.

activities.text: Reserve your tickets online before leaving: the ticket offices no longer sell tickets on-site since a few seasons ago, and agencies buy up entire batches. On weekend days, traffic in the gorges can be gridlocked—arrive at opening at 8am.

The boat ride on the sandstone lake of the circuit lasts twenty minutes and is worth every crown—50 CZK per person. It's not spectacular in itself, but the angle of view on the cliffs from the water is unique. The boatmen propel with poles: gondola atmosphere in sandstone.

days_plan.itin_title: Northeast Bohemia: From the Giant Mountains to the Rock Cities, 9 Days
Bohemia: Prague, sandstone cities & thermal spas in 14 days

visits.text: Perched on a promontory between the Lužnice and Smutná rivers, Bechyně Castle was transformed into a Renaissance residence at the end of the 16th century for Petr Vok of Rožmberk. The tour, in the spirit of his life, passes through the Palm Hall and its central pillar shaped like a tree. Curiosity: a gallery of children's portraits, painted in the belief that the child was doomed to die within the year.

Bechyně has lived from ceramics since the 15th century and is home to the country's oldest pottery school. The International Museum of Ceramics, at the castle entrance, and the Aleš Gallery in the former brewery display world-class pieces. Never crowded, the town has a drowsy rhythm.

activities.text: Cross the Bechyňská duha, Bechyně's Rainbow, a concrete bridge 50 meters high over the Lužnice, by car or historic train. Panorama over the whole town, and viewpoints below along the river. Beautiful walk on the Zářečí side.

days_plan.itin_title: South Bohemia: Český Krumlov, Castles & Ponds in 10 Days

visits.text: A ruined castle above, a Baroque château below, and between them, Czechia's second national treasure. Bečov nad Teplou Castle and Château hides the St. Maurus Reliquary in Pluhovský Palace, a 13th-century Romanesque goldsmith masterpiece. Found under a floor in 1985 by investigators, it rivals Prague's crown jewels.

The gold, silver, and precious stones of the reliquary are visible in a climate-controlled treasury room. The casket houses relics of St. John the Baptist, St. Maurus, St. Timothy, and St. Apollinaris. It even contains a 'toad stone,' a fossil fish tooth from the Cretaceous. Circuit I without reservation for individuals.

activities.text: Book Circuit II to discover the château's Baroque salons, the Beaufort library, and St. Peter's chapel. The view from the terraced gardens plunges into the Teplá valley. Allow 45 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: West Bohemia in 10 days: Spas, Castles & Beer

visits.text: In morning fog, Bezděz Castle floats in the clouds at 604 meters altitude - a silhouette that regularly inspires photographers from across Europe. Built by Ottokar II of Bohemia from 1264 and never remodeled since, it's nicknamed 'the king of castles' for its intact primitive Gothic appearance. The 13th-century chapel, royal palace, and panoramic tower are all accessible.

activities.text: If the sky is clear from Bezděz's tower, the view encompasses a good quarter of Bohemia. From Prague, the walk up from the village takes about 30 minutes on a marked trail. Open Tuesday to Sunday from May to September, weekends in April and October. Off-season, the exterior ruins remain freely accessible.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Bohemia: From the Elbe to the Rocks of Bohemian Paradise

visits.text: If Bor Castle is open, climb the 117 steps of the medieval cylindrical tower. The gallery at 30 meters offers a view of the Bohemian Forest and Přimda Castle in the distance. The Švamberk Hall, with its honeycomb vaults, and St. Lawrence chapel preserve a pair of handprints in the Renaissance plaster, according to legend those of two separated lovers.

activities.text: Cross the street to visit the Loreta of Bor, a Baroque chapel with a Black Madonna in its Santa Casa. The restored ambulatories display 18th-century ceiling paintings. Combined castle + Loreta tour: 70-80 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: West Bohemia in 10 days: Spas, Castles & Beer

places.name: Brno

visits.text: Špilberk Castle, founded around 1277 by Přemysl Otakar II, was transformed into a Baroque fortress in the 17th century then into Central Europe's most dreaded prison from the 18th century. Italian writer Silvio Pellico spent eight years here and wrote My Prisons. The Cathedral of St. Peter and Paul, whose twin spires reach 84 meters, hides a legend: in 1645, defenders rang the noon bells an hour early, fooling the Swedes. Since then, they ring noon at 11 a.m.

Villa Tugendhat, built by Ludwig Mies van der Rohe between 1929 and 1930, has been UNESCO-listed since 2001. Its large living room open to the garden, its golden onyx partitions, its motorized bay windows retractable into the floor were revolutionary. The Gestapo requisitioned it in 1939. In 1992, Václav Klaus and Vladimír Mečiar signed the separation of Czechoslovakia here.

St. James Church ossuary, discovered in 2001 beneath a downtown square, is Europe's second-largest after the Paris Catacombs: 50,000 bones—victims of plague epidemics and Hussite wars from the 14th to 18th centuries.

Punkva Caves, located in the Moravian Karst (the Czech Republic's largest karst system with over 1,000 cavities), allow access to the bottom of Macocha Abyss—138 meters deep, formed by the collapse of a cave ceiling in the 17th century. Two small lakes formed there, fed by the Punkva River whose underground flow is visible from two observation walkways. The stalactite named Romeo and the stalagmite Juliet, no longer fed by water for centuries and thus destined never to touch, illustrate the guides' imagination.

activities.text: Wander through the Cabbage Market (Zelný trh), a lively Baroque square that has hosted a market since the Middle Ages. In the evening, explore the bars and breweries in the Veveří student quarter.

Guided circuit through Punkva Caves (about 1h) followed by a motorboat crossing on the underground river between stalactite domes. Access to the Masaryk Dome—considered the most beautiful in the cave—is only possible by boat.

The Most Beautiful Places in the Czech Republic
Czech Republic Road Trip: Prague, Bohemia & Moravia

map_keywords: Cathedral of St. Peter and Paul Brno

visits.text: The inner courtyard of Broumov Benedictine Monastery strikes with its baroque severity—gray stone, round arches, imposing proportions. Christoph Dientzenhofer rebuilt the complex in the late 17th century after the destructions of the Thirty Years' War. The monastery also preserves a copy of the Turin Shroud—this 1651 copy is now kept at Břevnov Abbey, but a replica is visible during the visit.

In the cellars of Broumov Monastery, 34 mummies from the 17th and 18th centuries, including that of Magdalena Grambová, a Flemish baroness who founded the lace-making tradition in Eastern Bohemia. Above ground, the Benedictine brewery produces one of the country's oldest abbey beers—taste it in the cloister café.

activities.text: Drive out to the eight baroque churches of the Broumov Group: built between 1709 and 1743 by Christoph and Kilian Ignaz Dientzenhofer, they emerge isolated in the countryside like little baroque UFOs. The most accessible is St. Mary Magdalene in Božanov, 6 km north.

days_plan.itin_title: Northeast Bohemia: From the Giant Mountains to the Rock Cities, 9 Days

places.name: Ceske Budejovice

visits.text: A square spanning nearly 1.7 hectares with a layout so precise it's held up as a model: Přemysl Otakar II Square is one of the largest in Central Europe. At its heart stands the Samson Fountain and its baroque sculptures, whose originals now rest inside the town hall. Arcades ring the square, sheltering cafés and terraces. Come in late afternoon when the light gilds the façades and the tour buses have moved on.

Climb the 225 steps of the Black Tower and seven ancient bells await you at the top, each with its own name: Budvar, Maria, Marta. Standing 72 meters tall, the 16th-century Černá věž served as watchtower and belfry. The panorama over the rooftops and the Vltava is worth the effort, even if wire mesh gets in the way of your photos a little. Tickets are purchased at the summit; closed in winter, so check before you climb.

More discreet than the square, St. Nicholas Cathedral deserves a look for its baroque interior. More surprising still: the old Solnice, a 16th-century Gothic salt granary, now houses a motorcycle museum with dozens of vintage machines. The contrast is absolute. Allow twenty minutes for each; both lie at the heart of the old town, steps from the main square.

activities.text: This is where the true Budweiser was born, and the distinction matters: the Budějovický Budvar brewery offers tours in České Budějovice against the backdrop of a century-long trademark dispute with the American brand. The visit passes through the fermentation cellars and ends with an unfiltered lager drawn straight from the source. Book online—English-language slots fill up fast.

Walk the ring of canals that encircle the old town: České Budějovice is wrapped in waterways, remnants of its fortifications. A peaceful half-hour loop, lovely at sunset. No ticket required, just good shoes.

days_plan.itin_title: South Bohemia: Český Krumlov, Castles & Ponds in 10 Days

map_keywords: Přemysl Otakar II Square České Budějovice

visits.text: Český Krumlov Castle—the Czech Republic's second-largest castle complex with 40 buildings over 17 hectares—was founded in the 13th century by the Vítek family. Passed to the Rosenbergs in 1302 (their five-petal rose is the town's emblem), it was transformed into a Renaissance residence in the 16th century. Its moat has housed live bears since the 16th century. UNESCO-listed since 1992.

In the fifth courtyard, the Baroque theatre of 1765–1766 is one of only two in the world entirely preserved in their original state (the other is at Drottningholm, Sweden). Its stage machinery, painted sets, costumes, and orchestra pit date from the 18th century.

The Old Town, encircled by a meander of the Vltava, features cobblestone lanes lined with Gothic, Renaissance, and Baroque facades. St. Vitus Church, founded in 1309, houses the tomb of William of Rosenberg. The Egon Schiele Art Center chronicles the Austrian Expressionist artist's 1911 stay—his mother was from Krumlov.

Holašovice, 30 km from Český Krumlov, is a village of 140 inhabitants UNESCO-listed since 1998. Founded in the 13th century, it was entirely repopulated after a plague epidemic between 1520 and 1525 that left only two survivors. Its 23 farms with colorful stucco gables, arranged around an oval square, form the most complete example of South Bohemian Baroque rural architecture—a unique style fusing Czech and Austrian vernacular traditions. It's a living village, not a museum.

Zlatá Koruna Cistercian Monastery, founded in 1263 by King Přemysl Otakar II 8 km north of Český Krumlov, is one of Central Europe's best-preserved Gothic complexes. Its name ('golden crown') refers to the Crown of Thorns relic brought from Paris by the king.

activities.text: Canoe or kayak down the Vltava meanders around the castle—a loop of several kilometers offering unique views of the complex from the river.

Stroll through Český Krumlov Castle's Baroque gardens—11 hectares of terraces, a Rococo summer pavilion, and a cascade fountain featuring Neptune. The revolving open-air theatre installed in the gardens hosts performances during summer season.

visits.text: Český Šternberk Castle (hrad) has overlooked the Sázava since 1241. Founded by Zdeslav of Divišov, who drew his name from the star (Stern) on his coat of arms, it still belongs to the same Sternberg family nearly eight centuries later. Under austere Gothic walls hide Baroque salons and a library of several thousand volumes. Access is on foot from the river; bring good shoes.

activities.text: In fine weather, paddle the Sázava by canoe at the foot of the castle. The river threads through the forests of Posázaví, with a few livelier passages that entertain without real danger. The view of the castle from the water is worth the paddle stroke. Rent from a local club and check water level: too low in midsummer, some stretches scrape.

days_plan.itin_title: 7 Days Around Prague: Kutná Hora, Castles & Karst

visits.text: Underrated, Cheb deserves more than a quick stop. Cheb Castle, the only Hohenstaufen imperial palatinate in Czechia, preserves a two-story Romanesque chapel and the Black Tower. This is where Albrecht von Wallenstein was assassinated in 1634. Full price 120 Kč.

Touristy, the Špalíček? Yes, but these eleven 13th-century merchants' houses form a compact block without parallel in Bohemia. Slip through the Kramářská alley, barely 1.6 meters wide, that separates the two blocks. Free, five minutes is enough.

The echo resonates under the Romanesque vault of St. Nicholas Church, whose twin towers dominate the main square. The Gothic interior has been remodeled in Baroque, but the original structure remains legible. Free entry.

activities.text: Follow the Ohře River toward Krajinka Park for a quiet stroll away from the tour groups. The park offers sports activities in summer and views of the old bridge. Allow an hour round-trip.

days_plan.itin_title: West Bohemia in 10 days: Spas, Castles & Beer

visits.text: Chopin composed his Děčín Waltz within these walls during a stay with the Counts Clary-Aldringen - the Baroque-Neoclassical Děčín Castle has overlooked the confluence of the Elbe and Ploučnice for centuries. Residence of aristocratic families and military post successively, it has been fully restored and its interiors are open from April to October. The Baroque rose garden on the north terrace is a discreet gem.

The Shepherd's Wall (Pastýřská stěna) offers a view no one expects here: a 150-meter rock facing the castle, accessible by cable car or on foot, with a panoramic restaurant perched at the summit. The Elbe below, the castle opposite, the sandstone forests to the north. The surroundings abound in watchtowers accessible on foot from town.

activities.text: Go up to Děčín Castle in late afternoon for the Baroque rose garden, open in season (May-September). The Baroque sala terrena, the gloriette, and the view over the Elbe valley justify admission. In the evening, a walk along the river on the right bank offers the castle's reflection in the water.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Bohemia: From the Elbe to the Rocks of Bohemian Paradise
Bohemia: Prague, sandstone cities & thermal spas in 14 days

visits.text: The original parquet still creaks underfoot at Dobříš Castle (zámek Dobříš). This small Rococo palace from the mid-18th century belongs to the Colloredo-Mannsfeld family; its furnished interiors tell the lifestyle of the aristocracy. Tasty detail: under the communist regime, Czech writers came here to stay and write. Guided tour of the salons, then exit on the garden side.

activities.text: Two gardens answer each other at Dobříš: the French, geometric, and the English, wilder. The first unrolls geometric beds just behind the castle; the second, more untamed, invites the long stroll. The gardens rank among the country's finest. Admission is sometimes separate from the castle: check if your ticket covers both, and come in dry weather—the gravel paths handle rain poorly.

days_plan.itin_title: 7 Days Around Prague: Kutná Hora, Castles & Karst

visits.text: The Chods, historic guardians of the Bohemian frontier, left their mark on Domažlice. The 13th-century Chod Castle houses the region's oldest museum, with costumes, bagpipes (dudy), and ceramics. The elongated arcaded square is one of the most photogenic in West Bohemia.

The leaning tower of Chod Castle offers a vantage point over Domažlice's rooftops and the Bohemian Forest beyond. The Sladovna, a former malthouse converted into a gallery and microbrewery, deserves a beer and a look at the Hvozd gallery.

activities.text: If you pass in August, the Chod Festival animates the town with traditional music, embroidered costumes, and bagpipe competitions. Outside the festival, taste the Chod koláče, small pastries filled with poppy seed or curd cheese, in the center's bakeries.

days_plan.itin_title: West Bohemia in 10 days: Spas, Castles & Beer

visits.text: The only place in the world where the northern white rhino has bred in captivity, Dvůr Králové Safari Park concentrates Europe's largest collection of African animals—buffalo, giraffes, zebras in semi-freedom—across 72 hectares. Its Lion Safari, where visitors drive among lions without fences, is the only one of its kind in Central Europe.

A safari in Central Europe: the idea seems absurd until an elephant grazes your window. The Zdeněk Burian gallery, featuring the Czech prehistory illustrator, hides within the park grounds—unexpected and quite beautiful.

activities.text: Choose the Africa Truck over the Safaribus: the open flatbed truck lasts 60 minutes versus 25, and enters the Lion Safari. The Safaribus doesn't have access. Reserve your ride at the ticket office upon entry—spots go quickly in high season.

days_plan.itin_title: Northeast Bohemia: From the Giant Mountains to the Rock Cities, 9 Days

visits.text: Founded in 1793 by Emperor Francis I, Františkovy Lázně is the smallest of Bohemia's spa triangle. Its classicist center has been a UNESCO World Heritage site since July 2021 as part of the Great Spas of Europe. Twenty-one mineral springs gush across the town's territory.

If weather permits, the Salt and Meadow Spring Colonnade is the perfect spot to sample the waters. Each spring has a distinct taste, from salt to iron. The surrounding parks are beautifully maintained, with benches and flowerbeds. Bring your porcelain cup.

activities.text: Taste the water from František's Spring, the town's oldest, in its neoclassical pavilion. Touch the statue of little Františka, the town's mascot linked to fertility. The superstitious rub its toes.

days_plan.itin_title: West Bohemia in 10 days: Spas, Castles & Beer

visits.text: Nicknamed the most beautiful castle in Bohemia, and the acknowledged comparison is to Windsor: Hluboká Castle owes its white neo-Gothic appearance to the Schwarzenbergs, who remodeled a 13th-century royal fortress in the 19th century. 140 rooms, 11 towers, carved woodwork, and crystal chandeliers. The 52-meter tower can be climbed via 245 steps but closes in bad weather. Parking near the Penny Market, then a 900-meter uphill walk.

In the castle's former riding hall, the Aleš South Bohemian Gallery displays South Bohemian Gothic masters and Dutch painters. A short but dense stop, ideal if the line for the castle is long. Separate ticket from the castle tour.

activities.text: Stroll through the English-style park surrounding Hluboká Castle, designed by the Schwarzenbergs in the mid-19th century. Lawns, centuries-old trees, and views over the Vltava. Free, perfect before or after touring the interiors.

days_plan.itin_title: South Bohemia: Český Krumlov, Castles & Ponds in 10 Days

visits.text: Twenty-three painted farmhouses lined up around a long central green, not one more since the 19th century: Holašovice is the only Czech village inscribed on the UNESCO list, since 1998. The colorful gables in South Bohemian rustic baroque reveal themselves as you wander; count on fifteen to thirty minutes. The village was nearly emptied by plague in the 16th century, then repopulated by settlers. Come early: a single tour bus is enough to fill the square.

Push open the door of farmhouse No. 6, the Selský dvůr: the exhibition of South Bohemian peasant life gathers hundreds of objects from rural daily life. Small, but it brings the façades to life. Modest entrance fee, closed off-season.

days_plan.itin_title: South Bohemia: Český Krumlov, Castles & Ponds in 10 Days

visits.text: A mining camp since the 13th century, Horní Slavkov exported tin throughout Europe. The Museum, housed in a Renaissance building, presents the tin, silver, and uranium industry, plus a period bourgeois black kitchen. This is also where Bohemia's first porcelain manufactory was born in 1792.

The fortified church of St. George deserves a detour for its massive silhouette, rare in the region. It ranks among the most powerful fortified churches in Bohemia. Exterior access is free; for the interior, inquire at the town hall.

days_plan.itin_title: West Bohemia in 10 days: Spas, Castles & Beer

places.name: Hradec Kralove

visits.text: The Drawing Room of the Republic: that's what Czechs called Hradec Králové in the early 20th century, when Josef Gočár and Jan Kotěra transformed this medieval town into a laboratory of modernist architecture. The historic center, including the Great Square, is a protected national monument reserve. Arrive on a weekday to avoid the school groups that swarm the city on weekends.

White and slender, the Bílá věž tower dominates the Great Square from its 72-meter height. Built in the 16th century, it hides an illuminated glass model at its base that changes color. The 226 steps lead to a panorama over the Elbe and the Bohemian hills—count on 20 minutes for the climb.

Gothic from the 14th century and never baroquized, the Cathedral of the Holy Spirit is one of the few in Eastern Bohemia to have retained its medieval austerity. Inside, look for Petr Brandl's altarpiece and the pewter baptismal font. Free entry; a guided tour grants access to the bell tower.

activities.text: Walk north along the Elbe from the Great Square and you'll stumble upon the Hučák power station, an Art Nouveau watermill converted into a renewable energy exhibition center. Open March to October, entry around 80 CZK.

If you arrive on Saturday morning, the Great Square market sets up at 8am—cheeses, charcuterie, rye bread. The bike path along the Elbe heading south reaches Hradec Králové's urban forests in 45 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: Northeast Bohemia: From the Giant Mountains to the Rock Cities, 9 Days

map_keywords: Cathedral of the Holy Spirit Hradec Králové

visits.text: Hřensko, at the bottom of the gorge, is better than its reputation as a tourist village: it's the Czech Republic's lowest village, at the confluence of the Kamenice and Elbe rivers, ringed by sandstone cliffs and pine forests. Ideal starting point for all excursions in Bohemian Switzerland National Park (České Švýcarsko).

26.5 meters span, 16 meters high: Pravčická brána is Europe's largest natural sandstone arch, classified as a national natural monument. Narnia scenes were filmed here. Climbing onto it is forbidden—erosion is already at work. From Hřensko, count on 4 km on foot via the red trail or drive to Mezní Louka.

activities.text: The boatman speaks, the sandstone walls echo back: the Edmund Gorge in Kamenice canyon is crossed in a guided rowboat. The narrowest sections barely let the boat through, mosses and ferns brush your shoulders. Figure around 80 CZK. Departure from Mezní Louka.

Set off on the Gabriel trail from Mezní Louka to reach Pravčická brána via the heights: 2.5 km marked through the forest, with openings onto the sandstone landscape. Loop circuit possible via the red path on return, total about 7 km.

Hike from Hřensko to Pravčická brána via the red-marked trail (about 3 km, 90 min ascent). Then descend into the Kamenice gorges for the crossing by flat-bottomed boat, guided by a boatman with a pole on translucent waters between 150-meter walls.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Places in the Czech Republic
Northern Bohemia: From the Elbe to the Rocks of Bohemian Paradise
Bohemia: Prague, sandstone cities & thermal spas in 14 days
Bohemia: Prague, sandstone cities & thermal spas in 14 days

visits.text: Four hundred sandstone towers reaching up to 60 meters in height: Hrubá Skála is outsized, and every local climber knows the list by heart. This sandstone labyrinth perched in deep forest houses Hrubá Skála Castle, first mentioned in 1353 as the seat of the Valdštejns, rebuilt in Renaissance style in the 16th century then in neo-Gothic in the 19th. Its lookout points offer a plunging view over the labyrinth.

Local climbers know each tower of Hruboskalsko by name, and some routes bear poetic names: the Tower of the Moon, the Devil's Column. The area has climbing routes for all levels. For non-climbers, the marked hiking trails between the towers allow you to cross the rock city from one end to the other.

activities.text: Head to the Mariánská viewpoint for a panoramic view of the entire Hrubá Skála Castle and, in fine weather, the Ještěd watchtower in the Jizerské hory mountains. The Adamovo lože viewpoint is accessible on the Hrubá Skála-Valdštejn circuit. Bring hiking shoes.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Bohemia: From the Elbe to the Rocks of Bohemian Paradise

places.name: Jicin

visits.text: Prachovské Skály, 5 km northwest of Jičín, are the most famous rock formations in Bohemian Paradise (Český ráj), the world's first UNESCO Global Geopark in the Czech Republic. These sandstone columns and labyrinths formed over 100 million years ago in a shallow sea. Erosion has sculpted towers reaching 30 meters, narrow corridors, and rock windows. One gorge bears the name 'Emperor's Passage' since Emperor Franz I of Austria had himself carried through it by sedan chair around 1804 rather than walk.

Fifteen kilometers south of Prachovské Skály, the ruins of Trosky Castle are the symbol of Bohemian Paradise. Founded in the second half of the 14th century by Čeněk of Wartenberg, the castle was built atop two basalt volcanic plugs 47 and 57 meters high, christened Baba (the Old Woman) and Panna (the Maiden). Never conquered by the Hussites, it was finally burned by the imperial army in 1648 during the Thirty Years' War. Its ruined towers now offer panoramic views over the surrounding forests and rock cities.

Not far from Trosky ruins, the rock town of Hrubá Skála is a labyrinth of 400 sandstone towers reaching up to 60 meters in height, perched in deep forest. Hrubá Skála Castle, first mentioned in 1353 as the seat of the Valdštejn family, was rebuilt in Renaissance style in the 16th century and then neo-Gothic in the 19th century. Its viewing platforms offer sweeping views over this sandstone maze.

Valdštejnovo Square in Jičín, dominated by the 52-meter Baroque Valdice Gate tower, is the heart of this town that Duke Albrecht von Wallenstein intended to transform into his duchy's capital in the 17th century. The avenue of 1,200 lime trees planted by his order, stretching 2 km, leads from the gate to Valdice Castle.

activities.text: Hike through Prachovské Skály on marked trails. Three circuits are offered: yellow (1.5 km, 45 min, very accessible), red (2.5 km, 1h30, recommended for families), and green (3.5 km, 2h, most spectacular with the best viewpoints over rock towers and forests). At the entrance, look for the passage nicknamed Mouse Hole—a sandstone corridor so narrow you must squeeze through sideways.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Places in the Czech Republic
Czech Republic Road Trip: Prague, Bohemia & Moravia
Northern Bohemia: From the Elbe to the Rocks of Bohemian Paradise
Bohemia: Prague, sandstone cities & thermal spas in 14 days

map_keywords: Trosky Castle Ruins Bohemian Paradise Hrad Trosky
Hrubá Skála Castle Zámek Hrubá Skála

visits.text: Czechia's third-largest castle complex, 3.5 hectares, Jindřichův Hradec Castle was born on a 10th-century Slavic fort that became a Renaissance manor. Must-sees: the Knights' Hall and its 1338 frescoes on the legend of St. George, the 15th-century black kitchen, and the rare Mannerist music pavilion, the Rondel, with ingenious acoustics. Several guided circuits of about 50 minutes.

Beneath the covered galleries, the Laubengänge, the pastel-hued old town stretches around an oblong square by the Vajgar pond. A Jewish quarter and its Holocaust memorial hide within. Much quieter than Krumlov, you'll mostly encounter locals here.

activities.text: Hop aboard the narrow-gauge train from Jindřichův Hradec, in service for over a century, for an escape through the wooded "Czech Canada." Slow and old-fashioned ride, perfect with children. Check seasonal schedules before coming.

days_plan.itin_title: South Bohemia: Český Krumlov, Castles & Ponds in 10 Days

places.name: Karlovy Vary

visits.text: Karlovy Vary (Carlsbad in German) received royal town status from Charles IV in 1370, after the emperor discovered thermal springs here during a hunting trip. The town possesses 12 main hot springs and approximately 300 secondary ones. The most powerful, Vřídlo, erupts at 73°C with a flow of 2,000 liters per minute, forming a natural 12-meter geyser. Inscribed on UNESCO's World Heritage List in 2021 under 'Great Spa Towns of Europe,' the city has welcomed Goethe, Chopin, Schiller, Karl Marx, and Peter the Great beneath its colonnades.

Stroll along the Teplá River discovering the colonnades that line the spa center. The Mill Colonnade (Mlýnská kolonáda), built between 1871 and 1881 by Josef Zítek in neo-Renaissance style, is the most imposing: 124 columns support a 132-meter gallery sheltering five springs. Along the way, taste the thermal water served in small spouted cups—spa tradition dictates sipping it slowly to avoid irritating the stomach.

The Imperial Spa (Císařské lázně), completed in 1895 in French neo-Renaissance style by Viennese firm Fellner & Helmer, is the town's most monumental spa building, classified as a national cultural monument. After extensive restoration completed in 2023, its interior adorned with carved woodwork and murals depicting illustrious visitors from centuries past is once again accessible.

The Church of St. Mary Magdalene, built between 1732 and 1736 by Kilian Ignaz Dientzenhofer, is the town's most remarkable Baroque monument. Its dynamic facade and complex interior plan exemplify rare Bohemian Baroque. The neighboring Market Colonnade, an elegant wood-and-iron structure designed by Fellner & Helmer in 1882, shelters three springs including the legendary Charles IV Spring.

activities.text: Take the funicular up to Diana Observation Tower for sweeping views over the town nestled in its bowl of forested hills, then sample a glass of Becherovka—the 20-herb-and-spice liqueur produced exclusively in Karlovy Vary since 1807.
Indulge in a thermal spa treatment at one of the downtown establishments, which offer mineral baths, massages, and treatments using spring waters reaching temperatures up to 73°C.

visits.text: Karlštejn ticks every fairytale-castle box, yet everything is real. Charles IV founded it in 1348 to shelter the Bohemian crown jewels and his relics, on a cliff overlooking the Berounka. It's one of the country's most visited castles, hence crowded. The Chapel of the Holy Cross, with 129 panels painted by Master Theodoric, requires a separate booking. Buy your tickets online, especially for the chapel circuit.

activities.text: Climb on foot to the castle through the village of Karlštejn, lanes lined with shops and taverns. The climb takes about twenty minutes and warms the calves. Below, the hillsides carry an old royal wine tradition; a few cellars offer tastings of Czech wines. Leave the car below—access to the summit is forbidden—and park at the village entrance, which fills fast on weekends.

days_plan.itin_title: 7 Days Around Prague: Kutná Hora, Castles & Karst

visits.text: Perched above Kdyně, the ruins of Rýzmberk Castle belonged to the powerful Švihovský of Rýzmberk family. Only wall fragments remain in the forest, but the ascent (20 minutes) rewards with a clear view of the valley and the ridges of the Bohemian Forest. Free access.

The Borderland Museum in Kdyně, awarded in 2007 (Gloria musaealis), tells the story of frontier villages that disappeared after 1945. Among the pieces: 19th-century blue-print dies and embroidery from the Tšída factory. Small but well done.

activities.text: Make a detour to Kdyně's Water Fun Center if you're traveling with children. Pool, slides, and sauna in a human-scale complex, without the crowds of large water parks. Open in season.

days_plan.itin_title: West Bohemia in 10 days: Spas, Castles & Beer

visits.text: From the outside, Kladruby church seems Gothic. Push the door: it's Baroque. Jan Blažej Santini rebuilt this Romanesque basilica founded in 1115 between 1712 and 1726, inventing a unique hybrid style, 'Baroque Gothic'. The dome rises to 46 meters, crowned with a gilded Marian crown. Third largest temple in Bohemia after Prague Cathedral and Sedlec.

Cross through Dientzenhofer's New Convent, the other genius of Czech Baroque architecture. The contrast between Santini's original triangular plan (never realized) and the building ultimately constructed by Dientzenhofer is explained in the permanent exhibition on St. John of Nepomuk, a martyr directly linked to the monastery (1393).

days_plan.itin_title: West Bohemia in 10 days: Spas, Castles & Beer

visits.text: The spongy ground creaks beneath the wooden boardwalk of the Kladská nature trail, one of the oldest of its kind in Czechia (pioneering since 1977). The Kladské Peat Bogs National Reserve covers nearly 300 hectares of peatland above 800 meters altitude. Sundew and butterwort thrive here thanks to the ultra-acidic soil.

activities.text: If you arrive early, the little Kladská château and its pond offer a postcard-perfect setting. The Alpine-style buildings date from the second half of the 19th century, built by Prince Schönburg-Waldenburg. Trail of 2.4 km, twelve information panels.

days_plan.itin_title: West Bohemia in 10 days: Spas, Castles & Beer

visits.text: Beneath the Jesuit Church of the Immaculate Conception, the frigid air of Klatovy's catacombs has preserved mummies since the 17th century. The bodies of Jesuits and notables rest in glass-topped coffins, a spectacle between fascination and unease. Guided tour only, about 30 minutes.
Climb the steps of the Black Tower, 81 meters high, to overlook Klatovy and glimpse the Šumava in clear weather. The Renaissance tower is accessible in season for a few dozen crowns.

activities.text: The Baroque pharmacy At the White Unicorn, on the main square, is one of the best-preserved in Czechia. The original faience jars and furniture are intact. Quick but striking visit.

days_plan.itin_title: West Bohemia in 10 days: Spas, Castles & Beer

visits.text: Climb to the summit of Čerchov (1,042 m), the highest peak of the Bohemian Forest, accessible in 1.5 hours' walk from Klenčí pod Čerchovem. The former military surveillance tower at the summit offers a frontier panorama over Bavaria. Typical Chodsko village, with painted half-timbered houses.

days_plan.itin_title: West Bohemia in 10 days: Spas, Castles & Beer

visits.text: Czech hikers call it the country's most romantic castle. Kokořín Castle emerges from a sandstone spur above a forested valley, its round tower dominating the eponymous nature reserve. First written mention in 1320, built in the first half of the 14th century by Hynek Berka of Dubá - ruined during the Hussite Wars, restored from 1911 to 1918. The tower offers a plunging view over the dense forest. Interiors accessible May to September (60 to 120 Kč depending on tour).

activities.text: Walk up from the parking lot toward Kokořín Castle on the marked trail (about 20 minutes). The crenellated walls and inner courtyard with chapel merit the full circuit. If the castle is closed off-season, the exterior view alone is compelling.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Bohemia: From the Elbe to the Rocks of Bohemian Paradise

visits.text: St. Bartholomew's Cathedral (Chrám svatého Bartoloměje) dominates Kolín with its Gothic towers. We owe it partly to Petr Parléř, the architect of Prague's St. Vitus Cathedral, which places this Elbe town in a prestigious lineage. The bell tower opens for visits and serves as a viewpoint over the rooftops and the river. Check the belfry hours before climbing—they're restricted off-season.

In the silence of the old ghetto, the Renaissance synagogue of Kolín surprises. Founded at the turn of the 14th and 15th centuries, it has crossed the centuries with much of the Jewish quarter around it intact—rare in Bohemia. The interior, sober and bright, opens when a guardian is present. Ask at the tourist office that day: opening often depends on availability.

days_plan.itin_title: 7 Days Around Prague: Kutná Hora, Castles & Karst

visits.text: Over a mile long, the Koněprusy Caves (Koněpruské jeskyně) form Bohemia's largest underground network. You admire the "roses of Koněprusy," calcite concretions shaped like flowers, and guides recount that a clandestine medieval counterfeiter's workshop was discovered here. The site, under the Golden Horse (Zlatý kůň) hill, has yielded prehistoric remains. It's 50°F: bring a jacket, even in summer.

activities.text: Climb the Golden Horse (Zlatý kůň) hill after the cave visit. The summit overlooks the Bohemian karst and its old quarries; the open view rewards a short scramble. The spot is calm, far from the Karlštejn crowds nearby. Combine both in the same half-day, but check cave hours: guided tours only, by time slots.

days_plan.itin_title: 7 Days Around Prague: Kutná Hora, Castles & Karst

visits.text: Konopiště is less a castle than a frozen obsession. Archduke Franz Ferdinand, Habsburg heir, bought it in 1887 and crammed it with hunting trophies: his ledgers claim some 300,000 animals killed. He installed electricity, central heating, and an elevator—mad modernity for the era. Here he lived before his assassination in Sarajevo in 1914, the spark of World War I.

The rose garden at Konopiště unfolds its beds south of the castle. Laid out by Franz Ferdinand and his wife Sophie at the turn of the 20th century, it arrays hundreds of rose varieties, complemented by greenhouses with exotic plants. In June the fragrance is heady and the beds explode. Outside flowering season, interest drops sharply: time your visit late May through June to enjoy it fully.

activities.text: Walk to the lake through the 555-acre landscaped park surrounding Konopiště. Paths, statues, and ponds compose an English garden designed as scenery, far from the crowds massed before the castle. The leisurely loop takes a good hour. Park below, near Benešov, and walk up: the upper lot saturates fast on weekends.

days_plan.itin_title: 7 Days Around Prague: Kutná Hora, Castles & Karst

visits.text: The smallest spa in West Bohemia has kept an old-world charm. Konstantinovy Lázně specializes in cardiac rehabilitation and the musculoskeletal system. The spa park is modest but soothing, with a sulfurous spring known since the 16th century.

activities.text: Push on to the ruins of Gutštejn Castle, a few kilometers away. This romantic ruin, perched above the Hadovka valley, has free access. Beautiful forest walk of 30 minutes from the parking lot.

days_plan.itin_title: West Bohemia in 10 days: Spas, Castles & Beer

visits.text: The Kouřim open-air museum (skanzen) deserves the detour for one simple reason: it's one of the rare Czech open-air museums gathering peasant architecture from all of Bohemia. Created in 1972 to save houses threatened by dam construction, it lines up wooden farmsteads, a forge, and thatched cottages from the 17th to 19th centuries, furnished period-style. Count 45 minutes to an hour; late afternoon, low light, is most beautiful.

Built in the town's heart, St. Stephen's Basilica (kostel svatého Štěpána) hides two curiosities. Under the church, the Gothic crypt of St. Catherine opens to visitors; in the bell tower, bells mounted "upside down" ring in reverse—a rare case. The building dates to the Middle Ages, when Kouřim was a royal town more important than today. Limited opening: aim for weekends or ask at the neighboring regional museum.

activities.text: Sign up for a traditional craft workshop at the Kouřim skanzen. Several Saturdays a year, you learn an ancient skill and leave with your own object—more meaningful than a shop souvenir. The ethnographic programs follow the calendar of peasant festivals, so content changes by season. Check the program on the museum website before coming: not everything's offered every week.

days_plan.itin_title: 7 Days Around Prague: Kutná Hora, Castles & Karst

visits.text: Křivoklát Castle (hrad) hides deep in the royal forests west of Prague. Founded in the 12th century, it was the favorite hunting rendezvous of the kings of Bohemia, especially Wenceslas IV. Its great Gothic hall is one of the country's vastest, and the Fürstenberg library holds tens of thousands of volumes. The alchemist Edward Kelley was imprisoned here. Count about 100 minutes for the grand circuit.

activities.text: If the forest calls you, set out walking in the protected reserve of Křivoklátsko around the castle. These dense woods, kept wild for centuries as a royal hunting reserve, shelter rich fauna and flora. Trails follow the Berounka, beloved of canoeists. Bring water and a map: the trail network is vast and cell service sometimes absent.

days_plan.itin_title: 7 Days Around Prague: Kutná Hora, Castles & Karst

visits.text: A baroque hospital on the banks of the Elbe, in the middle of Bohemian countryside: incongruous, until you understand that Count Sporck wanted to make Kuks his personal Versailles. Built between 1707 and 1715, the hospital houses a three-hundred-year-old baroque pharmacy—with drawers labeled wolf liver, dragon's blood, and hanged man's powder. Guided tours only; English is available by email reservation.

On the hospital facade, twelve statues of Virtues and twelve of Vices, copies of the originals by Matthias Bernard Braun, known as the Czech Michelangelo. The originals are in the indoor lapidarium. In the forest 4 km away, the Braun Bethlehem: sculptures carved into living rock, of which only ten of the original twenty-six remain.

The Braun Bethlehem is a baroque nativity scene carved into sandstone rocks in the heart of the forest, commissioned by Count Sporck from Matthias Bernard Braun around 1726. The original program included twenty-six sculptures and reliefs; ten survive today, classified as a national cultural monument.

The Adoration of the Shepherds and the Arrival of the Magi emerge from the rock as if the forest had always borne these faces. The effect comes from the setting as much as the sculptures: an ordinary undergrowth, no sign announcing what you're about to find, then suddenly the bas-reliefs in the stone.

activities.text: Set out on foot on the 7 km loop to the Braun Bethlehem: the trail follows the Elbe then climbs into the beech forest. Hiking boots essential—the path is slippery after rain. Count on 2h30 round trip from the hospital.
From the Kuks bridge, follow the green trail markers: the complete loop is 7 km, 2h30 of walking. Hiking boots required—the path is slippery after rain. Information panels in Czech on each sculpture.

days_plan.itin_title: Northeast Bohemia: From the Giant Mountains to the Rock Cities, 9 Days
Northeast Bohemia: From the Giant Mountains to the Rock Cities, 9 Days

places.name: Kutna Hora

visits.text: The Sedlec Ossuary is an underground chapel within the Church of All Saints containing the bones of 40,000 to 70,000 people—victims of the Great Plague and Hussite Wars of the 14th and 15th centuries. According to legend, an abbot returning from Jerusalem in 1278 scattered earth from Golgotha across the cemetery, rendering it so sacred that thousands of families across Europe wished to be buried here. In 1870, woodcarver František Rint bleached the bones with lime and fashioned them into chandeliers, coats of arms, and garlands that entirely line the chapel—including a chandelier containing at least one specimen of every bone in the human body.

St. Barbara's Church, whose construction began in 1388 under Petr Parléř—the same architect who worked on Prague's St. Vitus Cathedral—is dedicated to the patron saint of miners. Its triple tent-shaped vault, a masterpiece by Benedikt Ried from the 15th century, and its frescoes depicting miners at work and coin minting testify to the wealth that earned Kutná Hora the title of Bohemia's second city, inscribed on UNESCO's World Heritage List since 1995.

The Italian Court (Vlašský dvůr), founded in the 13th century by King Wenceslas II, served as the Bohemian kingdom's principal mint for two centuries. Kutná Hora's miners then extracted a third of Central Europe's silver production, financing among other projects the construction of St. Barbara's.

activities.text: Descend into a reconstructed medieval silver mine beneath Kaňk Hill. The galleries dug from the 13th century extend as deep as 250 meters underground. Hard hats and miner's smocks are provided on-site.

visits.text: Lány speaks mainly to those interested in Czech history. The château is the presidential summer residence since 1921, but it remains closed to the public: it's the T. G. Masaryk Museum, facing the château, that recounts the first Czechoslovak president. Recreations, personal objects, trenches from the Great War. Open since 2000, it tours in an hour. Check opening days—variable off-season.

activities.text: Walk to Masaryk's grave in Lány's modest cemetery. The first president, his wife Charlotte, and his children rest there; under the communist regime, this discreet place became a symbol of democracy honored in secret. It's sober and loaded with meaning. Pair with a stroll along the Křivoklát forest edge before heading back toward Prague.

days_plan.itin_title: 7 Days Around Prague: Kutná Hora, Castles & Karst

visits.text: Metternich's cabinet of curiosities alone makes Kynžvart Castle worth the detour. The Austrian chancellor assembled personal objects from Marie Antoinette, a 3,500-year-old Egyptian mummy, and a cast of Alexandre Dumas's hand. The castle, built in 1690 and remodeled in Viennese classicism between 1820 and 1833, received the Europa Nostra prize in 2002.

Stroll through the castle's English park, designed by the imperial gardener from Schönbrunn. Three-hundred-year-old oaks stand beside a pond with an islet, an obelisk, and Májová Hill. The park has free access and takes about 1 km to tour.

days_plan.itin_title: West Bohemia in 10 days: Spas, Castles & Beer

visits.text: You'd think it a space capsule stranded on a mountain: Ještěd Tower (1,012 m) near Liberec is actually a 94-meter reinforced concrete hyperboloid designed by Karel Hubáček between 1966 and 1973, which earned its creator the Prix Auguste Perret for architecture. Restaurant, hotel and panoramic lookout over Bohemia, Poland and Germany. Funicular from Horní Hanychov.

Liberec Town Hall, completed in late 19th century neo-Renaissance style often compared to its Viennese counterpart, dominates the main square. Nearby, the 17th-century half-timbered Wallenstein houses form a striking contrast with the Radnice. Free exterior visit, museum inside.

activities.text: Take tram 3 to Horní Hanychov, then board the funicular up to Ještěd: 4 minutes of ascent, 400 m of elevation spared. In summer, several hiking trails descend toward the Jizerské hory Mountains from the summit. Round trip funicular: about 149 CZK.

days_plan.itin_title: Bohemia: Prague, sandstone cities & thermal spas in 14 days

visits.text: A village that no longer exists, yet weighs more heavily than many capitals. On June 10, 1942, the SS razed Lidice in reprisal for the assassination of Reinhard Heydrich. The 173 men over 15 present that day were shot on the spot; the women deported to Ravensbrück camp; the 105 children, mostly gassed at Chełmno. The memorial comprises the mass grave, the museum with the exhibition 'And the Innocent Were Guilty,' and Marie Uchytilová's bronze sculpture representing the 82 children murdered at Chełmno.

activities.text: Take time to read the names engraved in the bronze of the Lidice children's monument and walk through the rose garden planted with rose bushes gifted by cities worldwide since 1955. The garden and outdoor site are freely accessible; the museum charges admission (about 80 Kč adult). Closed Mondays.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Bohemia: From the Elbe to the Rocks of Bohemian Paradise

visits.text: Tourists are rare here, yet Litoměřice's medieval center is among Bohemia's best preserved. Founded around 1230, a royal town with 104 buildings listed as national cultural heritage, it's surrounded by 1,800 meters of Gothic ramparts largely in their original state. Mírové náměstí square, lined with arcaded houses including some of the oldest still inhabited in the country, is the ideal starting point.

Mírové náměstí square opens like an open-air Baroque theater: colorful arcades, town hall topped by its chalice-shaped tower - the symbol of the town's winemaking vocation. The tower is open to the public and offers a view of the confluence of the Elbe and Ohře. Steps away, St. Stephen's Cathedral dominates Cathedral Hill from its 50-meter bell tower.

The chalice tower atop Litoměřice's town hall is both the town's symbol and a practical lookout point. The regional gallery in the medieval castle displays works by Cranach the Elder, Karel Škréta, and Matyáš Bernard Braun - a collection few foreign visitors know about. Modest admission.

activities.text: Descend into Litoměřice's Gothic underground galleries, among Bohemia's most extensive. The tour route covers 366 meters and includes a lapidary and ethnographic museum. A local wine tasting is offered in the town hall cellars. Booking advised.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Bohemia: From the Elbe to the Rocks of Bohemian Paradise
Bohemia: Prague, sandstone cities & thermal spas in 14 days

visits.text: A prison until 1949, Loket Castle (Hrad Loket) has managed to make people forget its cells behind its porcelain collections, medieval frescoes and weapons. Founded in the late 12th century for the Přemyslid kings, this Romanesque castle—square tower, rotunda and bastions—overlooks a meander of the Ohře that surrounds the rock on three sides. Charles IV was imprisoned here as a child by his own father.

Loket's main square is surrounded by historic burgher houses with a baroque Holy Trinity column at its center. The village itself is classified as an urban reserve. 'Loket' means 'elbow' in Czech: look at the Ohře meander from the castle to immediately understand the name.

activities.text: From the lookout at Loket castle, the Ohře serpentines below in an almost circular meander, colored houses tier up on the bank. A short descent to the medieval bridge and a walk around the rock takes 30 minutes—and the castle silhouette above is worth as much as the interior.

days_plan.itin_title: West Bohemia in 10 days: Spas, Castles & Beer
Bohemia: Prague, sandstone cities & thermal spas in 14 days

visits.text: From the Máj viewpoint, the Vltava draws a dizzying meander below. The panorama plunges onto the Slapy dam lake, a reservoir some 25 miles long, filled in the 1950s. The concrete wall, about 210 feet high, is part of the "Vltava cascade." The trail is short but climbs steeply; wear decent shoes and keep an eye on children near the edge.

days_plan.itin_title: 7 Days Around Prague: Kutná Hora, Castles & Karst

visits.text: Goethe came for the cure, Chopin played piano, Edward VII took the waters—the main colonnade of Mariánské Lázně (119 m long, in neo-baroque cast iron from 1889) has received distinguished guests. The ceiling is decorated with a fresco cycle by Josef Vyleťal on the theme of mankind's desire to fly, added during the 1973-1981 restoration. Three springs inside, gently sloping garden behind.

The Singing Fountain (Zpívající fontána) of Mariánské Lázně, inaugurated in 1986, plays Mozart or Morricone every odd hour from May to October and lights up in the evening. The program is posted on a sign nearby. No ticket, no reservation—just come on the hour.

activities.text: Fill your glass at the Cross Spring (Křížový pramen), the resort's first spring, in its own whitewashed pavilion steps from the colonnade. It's open 6 AM to 6 PM. Bring a bottle or buy a souvenir mug on site—springs are free.

The Slavkov Forest (Slavkovský les), ten minutes from the center, changes registers completely: educational trails between pines and birches, geological park with rocks displayed along the path. An hour's walk is enough to escape the spa atmosphere and breathe.

days_plan.itin_title: West Bohemia in 10 days: Spas, Castles & Beer
Bohemia: Prague, sandstone cities & thermal spas in 14 days

places.name: Melnik

visits.text: Perched above the confluence of the Elbe and Vltava, Mělník Castle has belonged to the Lobkowicz family since 1793 - returned in 1992 after the fall of communism. From its terrace, the view encompasses both rivers simultaneously, the legendary hill of Říp, and the extinct volcanoes of the České středohoří. The interior displays the family's Baroque collections, notably paintings by Karel Škréta and Petr Brandl. The cellars extend over 1,500 m² at a constant temperature of 8 to 12°C.

In the crypt of the Church of Saints Peter and Paul, you discover with a certain vertigo one of the Czech Republic's largest ossuaries: the remains of some 15,000 people are displayed there, victims of plague and the Hussite Wars. The church itself, primarily Gothic, is mentioned as early as the 11th century. Its bell tower offers a view of the confluence. Enter through a side door, signposted - look for it.

activities.text: Descend into the Lobkowicz castle cellars to taste Ludmila, Mělník's signature white wine. Vines have been planted on the slopes beneath the castle for centuries - legend attributes their introduction to Princess Ludmila. Tasting is offered as an option with the tour; book ahead in high season.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Bohemia: From the Elbe to the Rocks of Bohemian Paradise
Bohemia: Prague, sandstone cities & thermal spas in 14 days

map_keywords: Church of Saints Peter and Paul Mělník

visits.text: The ridge of the Broumov Walls drops on one side in 100-meter cliffs over the Broumov valley, and descends gently on the other toward Poland. For 12 km, the trail crosses gorges, sandstone mushrooms, and narrow passages. Outside of Hvězda, you rarely encounter other hikers.

At the center of the national reserve, the Chapel of Our Lady of the Snows on the Hvězda hillock was designed by Kilian Ignaz Dientzenhofer and completed in 1733—on a star-shaped plan, hence the name. Next to it, a mountain chalet built in 1856 in Swiss style serves as a restaurant and trail junction point.

activities.text: From Hvězda, take the red trail toward Kovářova rokle: a 2 km long gorge, bordered by 100-meter sandstone cliffs, and only 2 meters wide in places. The Mariánská Cave is at the bottom. Count on 2h for the loop from Hvězda. Paid parking.

days_plan.itin_title: Northeast Bohemia: From the Giant Mountains to the Rock Cities, 9 Days

visits.text: Mikulov's Baroque castle dominates the town from a limestone spur. Rebuilt in the 17th century under Cardinal Franz von Dietrichstein, it was burned during the German troop withdrawal in April 1945. Restored after the war, it now houses Moravia's most comprehensive wine exhibition, as well as an enormous 18th-century wine barrel. Napoleon made a brief stop here on December 12, 1805, traveling from Brno to Vienna after the Battle of Austerlitz.

Mikulov's Jewish Cemetery, with its 4,000 graves dating from the 15th century, is the Czech Republic's second-largest after Prague. For 300 years, Mikulov was the seat of Moravia's rabbis. The Jewish community—up to 1,500 people in the 19th century—was entirely deported and exterminated during World War II.

The Pálava Hills, a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve since 1986, extend north of Mikulov on white limestone ridges where endemic plant species grow. From the Holy Hill trail (Svatý kopeček), a Baroque Way of the Cross installed in 1622 by Cardinal Dietrichstein to give thanks for the end of a plague epidemic, the view embraces Mikulov, the vineyards, and on clear days, the Austrian Alps.

activities.text: Taste Moravian wines in a Mikulov cellar—the region concentrates 5,000 of the Czech Republic's 18,000 hectares of vineyards. Typical grape varieties from the Mikulov sub-region are Welschriesling, Grüner Veltliner, and Pálava, a local variety producing aromatic, full-bodied wine.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Places in the Czech Republic

visits.text: The Škoda Museum is much more than a brand museum. In 1,800 m² of former production workshops, it traces 130 years of automotive history through restored vehicles, prototypes, and rare archives. The 1905 Voiturette, interwar sedans, and rally models are displayed side by side. Laurin & Klement began with bicycles in 1895 before moving to motorcycles then cars. Open daily 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. (about 100 Kč adult).

activities.text: If you like assembly lines, book a factory tour in addition to the museum - the production tour (about 2h in a group) is by advance booking and shows the assembly of current models. Availability varies depending on production days. Inquire at the museum ticket office.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Bohemia: From the Elbe to the Rocks of Bohemian Paradise

visits.text: A Baroque castle that smells of the 18th century - yet its history begins with a decapitation. Václav Budovec, owner of the estate, figured among the 27 Czech lords executed in Old Town Square in 1621; Albrecht von Wallenstein recovered the castle as a reward. Becoming Baroque between 1697 and 1703, the complex houses a historic theater with original sets, a library of 22,000 volumes, and the funeral chapel where Wallenstein's remains rest.

The library not to miss here: 22,000 volumes transferred from Duchcov Castle, belonging to the Wallensteins, but catalogued by Giacomo Casanova who was librarian there until his death in 1798. The room still exudes something of an Enlightenment cabinet of curiosities. Three tour circuits are offered.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Bohemia: From the Elbe to the Rocks of Bohemian Paradise

visits.text: Five courtyards, a cylindrical medieval tower, and a dominant position on the Polish border since the 13th century: Náchod Castle has passed through the Piccolomini, Courland, and Schaumburg-Lippe dynasties without losing its character. It successively belonged to the Italian Piccolomini, the Courlanders, and the Schaumburg-Lippe princes—each family left its layer in the interiors: Brussels tapestries, portraits from the Piccolomini gallery, aristocratic medals. Three separate tour circuits.

Josef Škvorecký, born in Náchod in 1924, renamed his hometown 'Kostelec' in his novels. Exiled to Canada after 1968, he founded a publishing house there for censored Czech literature. A statue of him sitting on a bench has been waiting since 2014 on Masaryk Square—with an empty spot next to him.

activities.text: Climb to the ochoz of the castle's cylindrical tower to see Náchod and the Polish plain—separate ticket to request at the ticket office. On the way down, stop by the Gallery of Fine Arts at the foot of the castle: 19th-century Russian painting and Czech art, fully accessible at ground level.

days_plan.itin_title: Northeast Bohemia: From the Giant Mountains to the Rock Cities, 9 Days

visits.text: An Italian villa set amid Czech fishponds, ringed by moats: Kratochvíle Castle was built at the end of the 16th century by William of Rožmberk as a pleasure pavilion. The showpiece remains the Golden Hall, with its lunette vault and mythological stuccowork by Antonio Melana, still used for concerts thanks to its acoustics. Open April to October, parking 200 meters from the gate.

Peacocks roam free and swans glide in the moat: Kratochvíle's geometric Renaissance garden lends itself to strolling. Inside, an exhibition on Czech animation and puppet cinema (Trnka, Zeman) surprises in this ancient setting. Count on a good hour in all.

days_plan.itin_title: South Bohemia: Český Krumlov, Castles & Ponds in 10 Days

visits.text: Two drawbridges and one of the deepest moats in the country guard Nové Hrady Castle, founded in the 13th century by the Vítkovec family. The courtyard is free to visit; the interior and its Buquoy portraits require a guide. The tower was blown away in 1573 by lightning and a gunpowder explosion. Open year-round, parking around the square.

East of the square, the Empire-style château of Nové Hrady was completed in 1806 by Theresa Buquoy. Three-wing building, grand staircase on the garden side. It now houses a branch of the Academy of Sciences; the exterior can be seen in passing.

activities.text: Descend into Terčino údolí, Theresa's Valley, a landscape park laid out in the 18th century at Countess Buquoy's request. Artificial waterfall, romantic follies, 7-km educational trail marked with 12 stations. National natural monument since 1949, ideal for stretching your legs.

days_plan.itin_title: South Bohemia: Český Krumlov, Castles & Ponds in 10 Days

places.name: Nove Mesto Nad Metuji

visits.text: From the outside, everything suggests Renaissance: sgraffito, round tower, stone arcade. Inside, architects Dušan Jurkovič and Pavel Janák redid the salons in Art Nouveau, Art Deco, and even Cubist style for the Bartoň family in the early 20th century. Nové Město nad Metují Castle, founded in 1501, ranks among the most unexpected aristocratic interiors in Bohemia: central heating and telephone at the dawn of the 20th century.

The castle's terraced gardens descend toward the Metuje in several tiers connected by a covered wooden bridge—English countryside atmosphere on a Czech river. In the corner of the upper garden, a few baroque statues from Braun's workshop. The park is freely accessible; the castle can be visited from May to September.

activities.text: Climb the round tower for a view of Hus Square (50 CZK on top of the castle ticket), then descend into the old town: a few cafés and an artisan grocery hide there. Count on 2h for castle + gardens + old town.

days_plan.itin_title: Northeast Bohemia: From the Giant Mountains to the Rock Cities, 9 Days

map_keywords: Hus Square Nové Město nad Metují

visits.text: The Holy Trinity Column, erected in 1740 at the end of a plague epidemic and consecrated in 1754 in the presence of Empress Maria Theresa, is the most monumental example of this monument type in Central Europe. Rising 35 meters high, adorned with religious sculptures by Moravian artist Ondřej Zahner, it houses a small chapel at its base. UNESCO-listed since 2000. Its square, Horní náměstí, is also graced with six Baroque fountains dedicated to mythological gods (Neptune, Hercules, Jupiter, Triton, Caesar, Mercury)—a unique ensemble recalling the legend that Olomouc was founded by Julius Caesar.

St. Wenceslas Cathedral, whose neo-Gothic towers reach 100 meters (the second-tallest in the Czech Republic), was founded in 1104–1107 by Prince Svatopluk. It witnessed tragedy in 1306: the assassination within its walls of King Wenceslas III, the last Přemyslid, ending the oldest Czech dynasty. Beethoven composed his Missa Solemnis for this cathedral in 1823; Mozart, taking refuge here in 1767 to flee a smallpox epidemic in Vienna, composed his Symphony in F major.

The Town Hall astronomical clock of Olomouc is the most original in the Czech Republic: designed in socialist realist style after 1945 (the original was damaged by the Germans in 1945), it depicts not Apostles but Czech workers, scientists, and athletes parading every hour.

activities.text: Sample Olomoucké tvarůžky, ripened cheeses typical of Olomouc with a very pronounced smell and taste, in a traditional downtown restaurant. These small farmhouse cheeses have been produced in the region since the 15th century and are a protected designation.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Places in the Czech Republic

places.name: Pec Pod Snezkou

visits.text: Pec pod Sněžkou is functionally a ski resort like any other—hotels, lifts, equipment shops. What sets it apart: it's the only starting point for the direct cable car to Sněžka, at 824 meters of elevation gain. The village itself can be toured in thirty minutes; all the interest is up there.

If you go up by cable car, descend on foot via the red trail through Obří důl: the descent into this former glacial valley takes 2h15 and shows characteristic striated rocks. The cable car (lanovka) doesn't accept reservations—expect a queue on fine-weather days in high season.

At 1,603 meters, you'd expect solitude. Instead: a post office, a bar, an observatory, and an international border running through the middle of the rock. Sněžka is the highest point in the Czech Republic and Poland's Lower Silesian Voivodeship—two countries literally share the same summit.

The Czech building Anežka—its name comes from an architecture competition where candidates had to choose female first names—looks like a spaceship moored on the rock. Inside, the Czech Post office stamps every postcard with a Sněžka seal. On the Polish side, the circular observatory and St. Lawrence Chapel evoke mountain stations from another era.

activities.text: Bring a waterproof jacket and closed shoes before going up to Sněžka: the average annual temperature at the summit is 0.2°C and the wind can blow hard even in midsummer. If storms are forecast, the ascent is discouraged even if the sky looks clear from Pec.

Send a postcard from the post office in Anežka: it's the highest post office in the Czech Republic. The Sněžka stamp is distinctive enough to be worth the postal detour. The souvenir shop is small, but the bar serves hot drinks that are precious in strong winds.

map_keywords: Sněžka cable car Pec pod Sněžkou

visits.text: The oldest preserved bridge in Czechia, predating Prague's Charles Bridge, Písek's Stone Bridge has spanned the Otava for 110 meters since the 13th century. Pedestrian, classified as a national monument, it has survived floods, submerged in 2002 then quickly restored. You can cross it freely year-round. Ideal light in late afternoon from the riverbank.

Písek's royal castle, founded by Přemysl Otakar II on gold-bearing sands, houses the Museum of the Prácheň Region. King's Square lines up pastel houses and the town hall under arcades. Písek was, in 1887, the first town in Bohemia to be permanently electrically lit. Allow half a day for the old town.

activities.text: Follow the Otava's bank from the stone bridge: the "rich river" carried the gold that made Písek's fortune. You can still try gold-panning for fun, and in summer a sand-sculpture exhibition sets up on the west bank. Easy walk, ideal for closing out the circuit.

days_plan.itin_title: South Bohemia: Český Krumlov, Castles & Ponds in 10 Days

places.name: Plzen

visits.text: St. Bartholomew's Cathedral, whose tower reaches 102 meters, is the Czech Republic's tallest church tower. It dominates Republic Square (náměstí Republiky), the country's largest medieval square after Prague. Inside, the Madonna of Plzeň (circa 1390) is a particularly precious Gothic sculpture.

The Great Synagogue of Plzeň, built in 1893, is the world's third-largest synagogue after those in Budapest and New York. Its facade blends Moorish and Romanesque styles, with two 50-meter towers. It can accommodate 2,000 worshippers.

The Pilsner Urquell Brewery, founded in 1839 by Plzeň's burghers, produced its first brew on October 5, 1842, under the direction of Bavarian brewmaster Josef Groll. By combining local soft water, Saaz hops, and pale malt, Groll invented that day the world's first pale lager—a style that today represents over 80% of global beer production. The name Pilsner Urquell, meaning 'original source from Pilsen,' was trademarked in 1898 to combat proliferating imitations. Tour the historic cellars dug beneath the city where beer still fermented in beech casks.

activities.text: Join a guided brewery tour to see the traditional triple-decoction brewing process and taste unfiltered Pilsner Urquell drawn directly from the barrel—the only chance to experience the beer before filtration and pasteurization.
Descend into the town's historic underground—a network of cellars and passages carved beneath the streets since the 13th century—and visit the Brewery Museum housed in a medieval Gothic malthouse.

visits.text: Police nad Metují is small, but its Masaryk Square—classified as a municipal monument reserve since 1990—is a pleasant surprise: Renaissance arcades, the Julinka fountain with water directly drinkable from the underground aquifer of the Polické křídové pánve. Thirty minutes is enough to walk around it.

The tabletop mountain Ostaš, at 700 meters elevation and 3 km northwest of Police, hides a labyrinth of monolithic sandstone blocks on its plateau—more than 500 marked climbing routes. The view from the Krtičkova lookout embraces Adršpach, the Broumov Walls, and on clear days, the Krkonoše.

The tabletop mountain Ostaš rises to 700 meters and dominates the Police plain by 250 meters. Its plateau shelters a maze of monolithic sandstone blocks in improbable shapes, classified as a national nature reserve since 1956.

The Krtičkova viewpoint, on the eastern edge of the plateau, offers a panorama of Adršpach, the Broumov Walls, and the Krkonoše on clear days. The plateau hosts over 500 sandstone climbing routes for all levels.

activities.text: Loop the 3.5 km marked Ostaš circuit in 1h30—accessible to all levels. Paid parking at the Ostaš hamlet. Combine with the Merkur Museum in Police (construction toys, unique in the Czech Republic) if you arrive early—it closes at 5pm.
Loop the marked 3.5 km circuit in 1h30 from the paid parking at the Ostaš hamlet. Well-traced trail, accessible without specific equipment. The road to Náchod is then 20 minutes away.

days_plan.itin_title: Northeast Bohemia: From the Giant Mountains to the Rock Cities, 9 Days
Northeast Bohemia: From the Giant Mountains to the Rock Cities, 9 Days

visits.text: The façades on Prachatice's main square are so finely etched with sgraffito you'd think them fresh: the Renaissance old town, wrapped in its ramparts, ranks among the best-preserved urban ensembles in the country. Its prosperity came from the Bavarian salt trade, brought from Passau via the Zlatá stezka, the Golden Trail. Protected zone since 1981. Wander the cobbled lanes, terraces around the square.

Climb the 157 steps of St. James Church tower (kostel sv. Jakuba), 53 meters high, for the view over Prachatice and the Šumava. The wooden staircase is steep and creaky, the safety netting interferes a bit with photos. Small fee paid on the way up, bat colony included.

activities.text: Pivovar Prachatice, the town brewery, deserves a fresh beer break after the climb. No-frills atmosphere, gentle prices, terrace when the weather's fine. Good stopping point before getting back on the road.

days_plan.itin_title: South Bohemia: Český Krumlov, Castles & Ponds in 10 Days

visits.text: The Emperor's Passage at Prachovské skály bears this name because Francis I of Austria had himself carried in a sedan chair around 1804 rather than walk through - the gorge is so narrow. These sandstone formations 7 km northwest of Jičín are the most famous in Bohemian Paradise, the first UNESCO Global Geopark in the Czech Republic. The columns and sandstone labyrinths formed over 90 million years ago.

activities.text: Hike in Prachovské skály on the green circuit (3.5 km, about 2h) - the most complete with the best viewpoints over the sandstone towers and forests. At the entrance, spot the Mouse Hole, a corridor so narrow you have to squeeze through sideways. Three circuits: yellow (1.5 km), red (2.5 km), and green (3.5 km). Adult admission 150 Kč; on-site parking.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Bohemia: From the Elbe to the Rocks of Bohemian Paradise

visits.text: Old Town Square (Staroměstské náměstí) packs six centuries of history into a single plaza. In the corner of the town hall, the Astronomical Clock (Orloj) has chimed every hour since 1410: the twelve apostles parade past two windows while the crowd tilts their heads in unison. It's brief, a bit kitschy, but the mechanism remains one of the oldest still ticking in the world. Come before 9 a.m. or after 8 p.m. to dodge the selfie wall.

The cobblestones of Charles Bridge (Karlův most) gleam at dawn, deserted. Launched in 1357 under Charles IV, this Gothic stone bridge served for centuries as the only crossing between the two banks of the Vltava. Thirty Baroque statues line it today; touch the bronze plaque of St. John of Nepomuk—tradition credits him with bringing luck. At 7 a.m. you'll have it nearly to yourself; by noon, it's a human river.

Prague Castle, founded in the 9th century on the left bank of the Vltava, is considered the world's largest medieval castle complex at some 750,000 square feet. Climb through the Hradčany quarter to reach the three successive courtyards, the second of which holds the National Gallery of the Castle and the third St. Vitus Cathedral.

St. Vitus Cathedral, begun in 1344 under Charles IV on plans by the Frenchman Matthias of Arras, was completed only in 1929—585 years of construction. His successor Peter Parler, summoned at age 23 to direct the project, invented net vaults of unmatched audacity for the era. Inside, the Chapel of St. Wenceslas is studded with semi-precious stones and 14th-century frescoes. The adjoining vault, locked by seven locks whose keys each belong to a different state dignitary, shelters the Bohemian crown jewels.

Golden Lane (Zlatá ulička), tucked against the north rampart of the castle, is a row of colorful little houses from the 16th century. Legend attributes the name to alchemists whom Emperor Rudolf II supposedly lodged here to transmute metals into gold, but archives show the actual residents were arquebusiers and craftsmen.

Descend toward Charles Bridge (Karlův most), whose first stone was laid on July 9, 1357 at 5:31 a.m.—a palindromic date and time (1-3-5-7-9-7-5-3-1) chosen by Charles IV's astrologer to guarantee the structure's longevity. Its 515 meters are flanked by 30 Baroque statues added between 1683 and 1714. Spot the bronze plaque under the statue of St. John of Nepomuk, a priest thrown into the Vltava in 1393 on order of King Wenceslas IV: touching the kneeling monk brings luck according to tradition.

The Church of St. Nicholas in Malá Strana, built between 1704 and 1755 by Christoph then Kilian Ignaz Dientzenhofer, is one of the masterpieces of Central European Baroque. Its 246-foot dome dominates the rooftops of the left bank. Mozart played the organ here during his Prague stays.

Old Town Square (Staroměstské náměstí) has been Prague's heart since the 10th century. On its south wall stands the Astronomical Clock (Orloj), installed in 1410 and reworked around 1490—the oldest in Europe still operating. Its dial represents Earth at the center of the solar system according to Ptolemy and simultaneously shows local time, sidereal time, and the position of the Sun and Moon. Every hour, the twelve apostles parade in the upper windows, framed by figures of Vanity, Greed, Death, and the Turk. Legend recounts that the city councilors blinded its creator so he would never reproduce this masterpiece elsewhere.

The Church of Our Lady before Týn, whose two asymmetric 260-foot towers have dominated the square since the 14th century, was the main Hussite church of Prague for nearly a century. The astronomer Tycho Brahe, court astronomer to Emperor Rudolf II, is buried there.

Explore Josefov, Prague's former Jewish ghetto. The Old-New Synagogue, built around 1270 in Cistercian Gothic style, is the oldest synagogue in Central Europe still in use. It is linked to Rabbi Löw (1520-1609), to whom tradition attributes the creation of the Golem—a clay creature animated by a magic formula to protect the Jewish community. Its attic supposedly still harbors the dormant Golem. In 1389, during the deadliest pogrom in Prague's history, more than 3,000 Jews were massacred in this quarter.

The Old Jewish Cemetery, founded in the early 15th century, is one of Europe's oldest. For lack of space, the dead were buried in layers up to 12 deep, explaining the spectacular crowding of some 12,000 gravestones. Rabbi Löw's tomb is perpetually covered with small prayer notes.

In the afternoon, excursion to Karlštejn Castle, 19 miles southwest of Prague. Built between 1348 and 1365 on plans by Matthias of Arras, it was designed by Charles IV as a strongbox for the imperial crown jewels of the Holy Roman Empire. The 197-foot Great Tower houses the Chapel of the Holy Cross, whose walls are inlaid with semi-precious stones and adorned with 129 panels painted by Master Theodoric—the largest ensemble of Gothic panel paintings in the world. Charles IV himself entered this chapel barefoot as a sign of humility.
Discovery of the historic heart of Prague: Charles Bridge, Prague Castle, St. Vitus Cathedral, lanes of the Mala Strana quarter.
Stroll through the Old Town around Old Town Square and the Astronomical Clock.
Discovery of Prague's historic center, the "city of a hundred spires"
Visit to Prague Castle overlooking the city

activities.text: Descend into the medieval underground of the Old Town, beneath the town hall. These vaulted cellars were the original ground floors, buried when street levels were raised against flooding. The guided tour recounts executions and legends, far from the din of the square above. Count on about 50 minutes, and choose the option with tower access for the finest view over the rooftops.

In the evening, walk the Vltava quays between Charles Bridge and the Legion Bridge to see the illuminated castle reflected on the river. The view from Kampa Island, accessible by a staircase under Charles Bridge, is particularly striking.
In the evening, settle on a terrace on Old Town Square to watch the Orloj apostles parade—the show repeats every hour until 11 p.m.

Return to Prague via the D1 motorway (125 miles, ~2 hours). If time permits before the flight, a last stop at the Havelské tržiště covered market in the Old Town—active since the 13th century—to bring back Bohemian garnet, puppets, or local glassware.
